coredata.dc:description: Solar evaporation provides an effective solution to solve the scarcity of freshwater resources. Biomass-based solar evaporators have attracted significant interest, because of their environmental safety. However, due to the high latent heat of evaporation for water, most biomass-based solar evaporators exhibit low water vapor generation rates. In this work, we have developed a wood-based solar evaporator (CDs-Wood) by just one step in-situ synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) using the lignin in wood as the carbon source, and the latent heat of evaporation for water in CDs-Wood has significantly decreased from 2257.5 J g− 1 to 1003.8 J g− 1, which has resulted in a water vapor generation rate as high as 2.86 kg m− 2 h− 1. CDs-Wood exhibits extensive optical absorption capacity (95.3 %), high solar steam conversion efficiency (80 %), significant adaptability to low-temperature conditions, and excellent salt resistance. This work provides an efficient and potentially scalable solution for designing all-biomass-based solar steam evaporators.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient solar water evaporation by wood through one-step in-situ synthesis of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049 PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solar evaporation provides an effective solution to solve the scarcity of freshwater resources. Biomass-based solar evaporators have attracted significant interest, because of their environmental safety. However, due to the high latent heat of evaporation for water, most biomass-based solar evaporators exhibit low water vapor generation rates. In this work, we have developed a wood-based solar evaporator (CDs-Wood) by just one step in-situ synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) using the lignin in wood as the carbon source, and the latent heat of evaporation for water in CDs-Wood has significantly decreased from 2257.5 J g− 1 to 1003.8 J g− 1, which has resulted in a water vapor generation rate as high as 2.86 kg m− 2 h− 1. CDs-Wood exhibits extensive optical absorption capacity (95.3 %), high solar steam conversion efficiency (80 %), significant adaptability to low-temperature conditions, and excellent salt resistance. This work provides an efficient and potentially scalable solution for designing all-biomass-based solar steam evaporators.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the growth of population and the development of industries, the scarcity of freshwater resources has emerged as a global issue [1–3]. Utilizing solar energy to assist in seawater evaporation is considered a significant approach to tackling this issue, as it not only utilizes abundant and renewable resources but also has a negligible environmental impact [4,5]. Nonetheless, the inadequate light absorption of seawater drastically hinders its evaporation rate [6]. To efficiently utilize solar energy for water evaporation, a “water-air interface evaporation system” has been developed. The materials used in this system usually have efficient photothermal conversion abilities and can proficiently absorb solar energy [7–10]. Additionally, this system should have exceptional insulation capabilities that can limit heat dissipation at the water-air interface [11,12]. The pore structures utilized to transfer water are also crucial, as they can continuously replenish water for the photothermal interface. Notably, metal nanomaterials [13,14], semiconductor materials [15,16], polymer materials [17,18], and carbon-based materials [19,20] are commonly applied as the interface evaporation materials, demonstrating remarkable performance of seawater desalination. However, as freshwater resources are closely linked to human life and health, opting for safer and more efficient materials to fabricate the “water-air interface evaporation system” is undoubtedly a must.

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs), a type of carbon-based nanomaterial, have attracted significant attention owing to their tunability and versatility, high photothermal efficiency, low toxicity, biocompatibility, cost-effectiveness, environmental friendliness, and excellent stability [21–25]. Many studies have shown that CDs exhibit exceptional photothermal effects and have been extensively used as photo-thermal agents for cancer treatments [26,27]. In addition to the ability as photo-thermal agents for cancer therapy, the CDs were also used as photo-thermal material for solar water heating system [28]. Using natural substances as carbon sources to synthesize CDs and employing them for seawater desalination can contribute to a green and sustainable method for obtaining fresh water. There are reports suggesting that CDs possess the capability to serve as photothermal conversion agents for the production of freshwater. In 2018, Pal and his colleagues were the first to report the application of CDs in solar evaporators [29]. The distribution of CDs in an aqueous solution remarkably enhanced the evaporation rate of water, which was recorded to be 25.5 mL dm−3 min−1. Subsequently, researchers have employed various approaches like heteroatom doping, functional group adjustments, and composite with other materials to improve the photothermal conversion efficiency of CDs, consequently increasing the water evaporation rate [30,31]. In 2020, Zheng and his colleagues succeeded in developing a solar evaporator, which exhibited the highest photothermal conversion efficiency of 97.1 % in CDs-based solar evaporators and a water evaporation rate of 1.7 kg m−2 h−1 [32]. Overall, CDs have great potential in the field of solar evaporators, however, they cannot form self-supporting materials with large surface areas.

Wood is a renewable resource in nature, exhibiting several advantages, such as the wood has anisotropic microporous channels that can transport water effectively, as well as quite low thermal conductivity of the wood that can greatly reduce the heat loss [33–36]. In addition, wood can reduce the enthalpy of evaporation by changing the water state when water evaporates. This method has been reported and applied by a few researchers to improve the evaporation rate of water [37,38]. Tang et al. prepared a wood-based evaporator with reduced enthalpy of evaporation by carbonizing wood. This research showed that capillary water in wood evaporates in the form of clusters, resulting in a decreased evaporation enthalpy. Zhang's research showed that reducing the pore size of the wooden tube cavity helps to reduce the enthalpy of water evaporation. This further confirms the cluster evaporation mode can be well applied in wood-based evaporators. Furthermore, the hydrophilic groups presented on the surface of wooden tube cavity can decrease the enthalpy of evaporation through increasing non-covalent interactions with water molecules. Consequently, enhancing the hydrophilicity of wood can lead to a reduction in the enthalpy of water evaporation. This phenomenon has been applied by Lu et al. to construct an efficient wood/organic covalent framework composite solar evaporator, which has a low evaporation enthalpy due to its excellent hydrophilicity [39]. In summary, wood can be applied as an ideal matrix for the preparation of solar evaporators.

Due to the excellent properties of wood and CDs, people have been considering to build evaporators by using CDs as the solar absorbers and wood as the supporting materials. The evaporators made from CDs and wood composites materials have many advantages, such as: high photothermal efficiency, tunability and versatility, low toxicity and environmental friendliness, and sustainability. Hou and his colleagues constructed evaporators by assembling CDs in the micropores of wood [40]. Their results showed that micropores in wood can not only rapidly transport water but also reduce the enthalpy of water evaporation, at the same time, the CDs provide good photothermal conversion capability for the evaporator and reached an evaporation rate of 2.27 kg m− 2 h− 1. Recently, Zhang and his colleagues used xylan as a carbon source to prepare CDs through hydrothermal reactions, then built an evaporator by dispersing these CDs into the wood through immersing the wood in CDs suspension, and the water evaporation efficiency of this evaporator can reach 3.39 kg m− 2 h− 1 [41]. Although CDs and wood composite evaporators have been reported, these works involve preparing CDs first and then incorporating them with the wood. Such preparation processes of the evaporator are relatively complex due to a few separated steps.

In this study, we demonstrated that CDs-Wood evaporators can be prepared by only one-step in-situ synthesis of CDs directly in natural wood, and we just used the lignin of wood as the carbon source, no need to get or extract the lignin or other carbon source from elsewhere. Thus, we can make the process simpler, environment-friendly, and cost-effective. In contrast to previous approaches, which involved complex steps, such as preparation of carbon dots first, then incorporation of them with the wood by absorption or surface coating. The characterization of water evaporation performance shows that the composite CDs-Wood allows for continuous, efficient and stable solar interface evaporation. Under a light intensity of 1 kW m−2 (equivalent to 1 sun) and an ambient temperature of 20 °C, the water evaporation rate of this evaporator can reach up to 2.86 kg m− 2 h− 1 with a solar thermal conversion efficiency of 80 %. The high evaporation efficiency mainly comes from the effectiveness of CDs-Wood in reducing the latent heat of water evaporation (from 2257.5 to 1003.8 J g−1). Another notable feature of the evaporator is its exceptional adaptability to environmental conditions. Even at a chilly ambient temperature of 8 °C, the water evaporation rate remains as high as 1.74 kg m− 2 h− 1. Remarkably, regardless of whether applied to seawater or saturated saltwater, the water evaporation rate remains constant and equivalent to that of CDs-Wood applied for pure water. Furthermore, the CDs-Wood evaporator exhibits excellent stability and salt-resistant performance during long-term continuous operation and cycle-tests, without any salt accumulation on its surface while maintaining a stable rate of evaporation as high as 2.86 kg m− 2 h− 1.

As described in the experimental section and schematically shown in Fig. 1
a, CDs-Wood composites were prepared directly from wood using the hydrothermal treatment in just one step. The untreated wood was obtained by radial cutting of Balsa wood. After the hydrothermal reaction, the color of the wood was changed from light yellow to black, and its edge length was slightly decreased from 1.5 cm to 1.3 cm. In order to confirm the successful synthesis of CDs in wood, the spectra and structures of CDs and CDs-Wood were characterized and analyzed. Firstly, the prepared CDs-Wood was smashed and immersed in 10 mL of deionized water and then sonicated for 30 min. Then, the resulted mixture was filtered to obtain an aqueous suspension of CDs. The suspension showed brown color under sunlight and emitted blue fluorescence when exposed to an ultraviolet lamp (Fig. 1 b, insets). As shown in Fig. 1 b, the suspension exhibited a typical photoluminescence spectrum (PL) for CDs. When the excitation wavelength was increased from 260 to 500 nm, the emission wavelength gradually shifted to red, and the fluorescence intensity first increased and then decreased. The solution had the strongest fluorescence emission under excitation light of 320 nm, and the optimal emission peak was at 404 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the suspension showed that the absorption of CDs was mainly in the ultraviolet region and extended to the near-infrared region ( Fig. S1 ). This was mainly caused by a large number of C = C and C = N bonds in the CDs. Comparing the CDs within the wood to those present in the hydrothermal solution utilized for synthesizing CDs-Wood reveals that these two types of CDs are identical ( Fig. S2 ).

In addition, Raman spectrum and XRD investigations also indicated the successful formation of CDs inside of the wood. The crystal structure of the CDs was analyzed by XRD ( Fig. S3
), the sample had a wide diffraction peak at 25°, which was corresponded to the (002) crystal plane of graphitization carbon. The diffraction peak was caused by the graphitization core of CDs, which indicated that it had a high degree of graphitization. The graphitization structure of CDs was further studied by Raman spectra, as shown in Fig. 1 c (red line), the D-band at 1374 cm−1 represented the amorphous carbon and the G-band at 1576 cm−1 was due to the graphitized carbon. The ratio of the relative strength of the D-band to the G-band (ID/IG) is widely used to characterize the degree of graphitization of carbon materials [42,43]. The ID/IG value of the CDs was about 0.61, indicating that it had a high degree of graphitization, which was consistent with the XRD result. Meanwhile, Raman spectrum indicated the presence of amorphous carbon in CDs, which may be caused by defects in the carbon nucleus [44–46]. The untreated wood did not show obvious Raman peaks of CDs (Fig. 1 c, black line). However, the Raman spectra of CDs-Wood show significant carbon material peaks, with an ID/IG value of approximately 0.62, which is close to CDs (Fig. 1 e, blue line). These results confirmed the formation of CDs inside the wood.

The morphology of CDs was measured by transmission electron microscope (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 d, the CDs are well-dispersed spherical particles. The good dispersion is due to their negative surface charges indicated by their Zeta potential ( −16.8 mV). The diameter of these particles is between 1.4 nm and 4.7 nm, with an average diameter of 2.8 nm. The morphology and distribution of CDs on the CDs-Wood was also investigated by TEM, as shown in (Fig. 1 e), a high density of spherical CDs was formed on a piece of the flake which was torn from the CDs-Wood. The diameter of these particles is from 1.8 nm to 4.7 nm, with an average diameter of 2.9 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the evaporation performance of CDs-Wood
ce:section.ce:para:
The evaporation test setup was built as shown in Fig. 2 a. The CDs-Wood based evaporator was assembled as shown in Fig. S9, a watch-strap shaped filter paper was placed on a polyethylene foam, then the CDs-Wood with a dimension of 1.3 cm × 1.3 cm × 0.5 cm was placed on the filter paper. During the tests, the two straps of the filter paper were immersed in water, and the area around the CDs-Wood was covered with foam to prevent additional evaporation of water. The weight loss was recorded using an electronic balance. The temperature of the CDs-Wood under 1 sun irradiation during the test was recorded by an infrared camera, as shown in Fig. 2 b, the temperature of CDs-Wood rapidly increased from 19.6 °C to 43.8 °C within only 10 min and remained in equilibrium thereafter (Fig. 2 c). The evaporation rate of water without any wood under 1 sun irradiation was measured to be 0.31 kg m− 2 h− 1, which is consistent with the data reported in literature (Fig. 2 d) [50]. The water evaporation rate with CDs-Wood under 1 sun irradiation at 20 °C was 2.86 kg m− 2 h− 1, which is much higher than that of untreated wood (0.63 kg m− 2 h− ­1). The water evaporation rate under 1 sun is a key parameter for evaluating the performance of the evaporators to generate vapour under ambient conditions. Therefore, the performance of the CDs-Wood was compared with the recently reported natural materials based evaporators, and summarized in Table S1 . The evaporation rate of the CDs-Wood exceeded that of most natural materials based evaporators, but only one evaporator had an evaporation rate of 3.3895 kg m− 2 h− 1, which was higher than our evaporator's evaporation rate of 2.86 kg m−2 h−1. However, the construction process of this evaporator requires the synthesis and purification of CDs from xylan first, and then incorporation of the prepared CDs into wood, which is relatively complex compared to the one-step construction process of our evaporator, and there is no need to get carbon source for CDs from elsewhere, since we utilized the lignin in the wood as the carbon source for the in-situ preparation of CDs. It is worth noting that CDs-Wood itself can float on the seawater, however, most of it was underneath the water, thus the heat conducted from the CDs-Wood to the bulk water was easily dissipated without the supporting foam, and the evaporation rate was only 1.81 kg m− ­2 h− 1 ( Fig. S10).The energy efficiency (η) for solar-vapor conversion of CDs-Wood was calculated to be 80 % by using equation (1), where m is the mass flux while reaching steady-state condition, P0 is the solar irradiation power of 1 sun irradiation (1 kW m−2), and Copt refers to the optical concentration on the absorber surface (characterized by using UV-Vis-NIR, 95.3 %), and Eequ is the latent heat of water evaporation of the CDs-Wood (1043 J g−1, by DSC measurements).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the morphology and composition of CDs-Wood
ce:section.ce:para:
The microstructures of wood and CDs-Wood were observed by SEM, as shown in Fig. 3
a, there are two different channels in the wood, of which the dominant small channels have a pore size of about 40 nm, and the wall thickness of these channels is about 200 nm (Fig. 3 a, insets). The large pores have a diameter of about 250 μm, but they are much less than the small pores in quantity. There is no significant change in the microstructures of CDs-Wood compared with the original wood after modifications (Fig. 3 b). The preservation of the pore structures in CDs-Wood is extremely important for its application in seawater desalination because these channels can continuously transport seawater to the photothermal surface. After hydrothermal treatment, gaps formed between the wood pore walls (Fig. 3 d, insets), mainly due to the consumption of lignin between the pore walls. Furthermore, the consumption of lignin weakened the internal mechanical integrity of the wood [51]. Consequently, during the drying process, the shrinkage occurred in the wood due to the capillary force from water evaporation, resulting in a reduction in its volume.

Wood and CDs-Wood were also analyzed by FTIR. As shown in Fig. 3 c, the peak at 1735 cm−1 of wood was due to the C = O vibration of aldehyde and ester groups in hemicellulose and lignin. But this peak in CDs-Wood was significantly weaker than that in wood. The peaks at 1593 cm−1, 1503 cm−1, and 1460 cm−1 of wood represented the C = C, C-O, and C-H vibrations in lignin, respectively [52]. The disappearance of these peaks in CDs-Wood indicated that lignin has been consumed during the hydrothermal treatment. Furthermore, the peak at 895 cm−1 was due to the absorption by guaiacol [53], which was a product from the lignin decomposition [52], thus, the presence of this peak at 895 cm−1 of CDs-Wood also indicated the consumption of lignin. However, the peaks at 1422 cm−1 and 1370 cm−1 were due to the bending and deformation vibrations of C-H of cellulose, respectively, thus the presence of these two peaks in CDs-Wood indicated that cellulose was still reserved after the hydrothermal treatment. Thus, we believe that the lignin in the wood was consumed during the preparation of CDs-Wood by hydrothermal treatment.

Generally speaking, the crystalline regions in wood are mainly formed by cellulose, while hemicellulose and lignin are in the amorphous regions. The crystallinity changes of wood before and after the hydrothermal reaction were also investigated by XRD. As shown in Fig. 3 d, the peak at 2θ = 18.7° (Iam ), which only represents the amorphous components. I002 is the maximum diffraction intensity of the (002) crystal plane at 2θ = 22.4°, which represents the total intensity from both crystalline and amorphous components. The crystallinity index is calculated using the following equation (2) [52 54]. The results showed that while the original wood was about 29.3 %, the crystallinity index of CDs-Wood was increased to 43.6 %, which might be due to the consumption of lignin in wood. Therefore, it is highly possible that lignin, as a carbon source, was carbonized in the wood to form CDs during the hydrothermal treatment.

To further explore the carbon sources involved in the formation of CDs in the CDs-Wood, three types of CDs were obtained by using cellulose, lignin, and hemicellulose as carbon sources under the same hydrothermal conditions and they were named as C-CDs, L-CDs, and H-CDs, respectively. Subsequently, fluorescence spectrum and TEM characterization were performed on these three CDs ( Fig. S15
). The results showed that the L-CDs prepared using lignin as a carbon source were similar to the CDs formed in CDs-Wood, since they have the comparable size and fluorescence spectra as shown in Fig. S15
a and b. The fluorescence of C-CDs obtained with cellulose as carbon sources, as well as H-CDs obtained with hemicellulose as carbon sources, both showed a red shift when compared to the wavelength of CDs in CDs-Wood ( Fig. S15
c, S15e). In addition, the diameter of C-CDs was greater than that of CDs-Wood, while the diameter of H-CDs is smaller than that of CDs-Wood ( Fig. S15
d, S15f). At the same time, the yield of L-CDs was much higher than that of H-CDs and C-CDs (which was 50 times higher than H-CDs and 200 times higher than C-CDs). The above results indicated that the carbon source of CDs in CDs-Wood was mainly lignin.

To further confirm the origin of the carbon source for CDs in CDs-Wood, the lignin content in wood was first reduced from 19 % to 3 % by NaClO2 chemical treatments according to a previously reported method [55]. The fluorescence intensity of CDs-Wood prepared from the wood with much lower lignin content (3 %) was also significantly reduced (Fig. 3 e). Additionally, the light absorption ability of this CDs-Wood was also decreased (Fig. 3 f). The decrease in fluorescence intensity and light absorption ability of such CDs-Wood was mainly due to the decrease in the quantity of CDs formed inside the wood with low-lignin content. Thus, lignin plays an important role as the carbon source for the CDs formation inside the wood. The main reason is that lignin has a high carbon content and a large number of aromatic structures, making it an ideal carbon source for CDs. Using lignin as a carbon source can easily obtain CDs through hydrothermal methods [56]. In contrast, cellulose and hemicellulose lack of active groups and cannot be easily cross-linked and carbonized to form CDs [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the evaporation mechanism of CDs-Wood
ce:section.ce:para:
The ability to absorb light is an important parameter for evaluation of an evaporator. The principal mechanism for converting sunlight into thermal energy by CDs is as follows: firstly, the surface of CDs comprises a considerable number of imperfect sp2 structural defects and functional groups, leading to a large number of nonradiative transitions and subsequent heat generation [58,59]. Secondly, the introduction of numerous heteroatoms enhances the amorphousness of CDs, thereby reducing their crystallinity and increasing the number of defect sites. This, in turn, promotes nonradiative transitions [60,61]. As shown in Fig. 4
a, the light absorption spectra of CDs-Wood and wood were collected in the wavelength of 200–2500 nm, the light absorption capacity was calculated using the following formula (3), and for CDs-Wood, it was 95.3 %, which was much higher than that of wood (56.1 %). Photothermal conversion ability also plays an important role in the evaporation process. The photothermal behavior of CDs-Wood was evaluated by measuring the temperature of their surface using an infrared camera under 1 sun irradiation. Fig. 4 b and 4c showed the changes in surface temperature of both dried wood and dire CDs-Wood without any water. The initial surface temperature of these two samples was about 23 °C, and the temperature increased rapidly and researched to the thermal equilibrium after 5 min. It is noteworthy that the equilibrium surface temperature of CDs-Wood was as high as 73.3 °C, which was significantly higher than that of wood (35.5 °C). This result indicated the fast light response capability of the CDs-Wood. Furthermore, this temperature (73.3 °C) was much higher than that at the evaporation interface during water evaporation (43.8 °C), signifying it can effectively convert solar energy into steam. Overall, CDs-Wood exhibits exceptional photothermal conversion capability, which allows efficiently convert solar energy into heat energy for water evaporation. The capability of water transfer is important for evaporators. The water transfer rate of this CDs-Wood was measured to be 35.2 kg m−2 h− 1, which was much more than the water evaporation rate obtained from our tests, thus the water transfer capability of our setup would not restrict the test of water evaporation.

The enthalpy of water evaporation also plays a crucial role in determining the evaporation rate, and it is influenced by both the state of the water and the evaporation process. The enthalpy of evaporation of pure water, water in wood, and water in CDs-Wood was determined using differential scanning calorimetry (DSC). As shown in Fig. 4 d, pure water displayed high and sharp absorption peaks, whereas water in wood and CDs-Wood displayed broad absorption peaks. Based on the DSC data, the enthalpy of evaporation of pure water was measured to be 2257.5 J g−1, which is close to the standard value of 2444 J g−1. The enthalpy of evaporation of water in wood was 1632.4 J g−1. The enthalpy of evaporation of water in CDs-Wood was further decreased and only about 1003.8 J g−1, which was close to the equivalent evaporation enthalpy of water (1043 J g−1) calculated by the following equation (4).

where E0 and v0 refer to enthalpy of evaporation of pure water and evaporation rate under the dark condition, respectively. And vg is the evaporation rate of CDs-Wood in the same environmental conditions (When calculating the evaporation rate, the evaporation area we used includes both of the projected area and the four sides of the CDs-Wood).

To explain the decrease in the enthalpy of water evaporation in CDs-wood, we referred to the water cluster theory and non-covalent interactions. Water can evaporate as a single molecule or as a small cluster composed of several to dozens of molecules, and the evaporation enthalpy of cluster evaporation is smaller than that of single molecules [62]. It has been reported that water in wood always maintained in a capillary water state, which tends to evaporate in the form of molecular clusters [37,38]. Thus, using wood as an evaporator can effectively reduce the enthalpy of water evaporation. A large amount of lignin was consumed during the preparation of CDs-Wood, at the same time, the generated CDs contain a large number of hydrophilic groups (such as –NH2, –COOH, –OH), which can greatly improve the hydrophilicity of CDs-Woods. Hydrophilic groups may interact with water molecules through non-covalent interactions, thereby reducing the enthalpy of evaporation [63]. Overall, the pore structures inside CDs-Wood and the enhanced hydrophilicity resulted from hydrothermal treatment collectively contributed to the reduction of the enthalpy of water evaporation.

To further investigate the water-water interactions in CDs-Wood, Raman spectra of water, water with wood and water with CDs-Wood were obtained. The peaks at 3233 cm−1, 3401 cm−1, 3514 cm−1, and 3630 cm−1 were due to water molecules [64], and these peaks can be classified into two modes: (1) water molecules with four hydrogen bonds, namely, two protons and two electron pairs, involved in hydrogen bonding (peaks at 3233 cm−1 and 3401 cm−1); (2) weakly or non-hydrogen-bonded water molecules, in which the hydrogen bonds of the water molecules have been partially or completely broken (peaks at 3514 cm−1 and 3630 cm−1). Water in substances can be categorized as free water (FW), bound water (BW), and intermediate water (IW). Compared to FW, IW requires less energy to be evaporated, which can lead to a decrease in the enthalpy of water evaporation [65,66]. In addition, there are two primary modes of water volatilization: as a single water molecule, where it must overcome hydrogen bonds interaction with adjacent water molecules, or as molecular clusters, which have less interaction to overcome and therefore exhibit a lower enthalpy of water evaporation [67]. The results presented in Fig. 4 e, 4f, and 4g revealed that the molar ratios of IW to FW in pure water, water within the wood, and water within the CDs-Wood were 0.34, 0.40, and 1.35, respectively (Fig. 4 h). The value of IW/FW for water in wood (0.40) was comparable to that of pure water (0.34), suggesting that the decrease in the enthalpy of water evaporation within the wood was not primarily due to interaction in water-water interaction but it is more possible due to the presence of anisotropic micropores in wood which induces water to be evaporated in the form of molecular clusters, resulting in an obvious reduction in the enthalpy of water evaporation from 2257.5 J g−1 to 1632.4 J g−1. The IW/FW of water within CDs-Wood (1.35) was much higher than that of water within wood (0.40), which indicated that the polar functional groups on the surface of CDs can weaken water-water interaction and produce a large amount of intermediate water within CDs-Wood. This, in turn, led to a further decrease in the enthalpy of water evaporation. Since the CDs-Wood also had similar anisotropic micropores as the wood, in which the enthalpy of water evaporation can also be further reduced, thus, the combination of these two effects contributed to a significantly low enthalpy of water evaporation of water in CDs-Wood (only 1003.8 J g−1), even less than half of that of pure water (2257.5 J g−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of CDs-Wood's resistance to low temperature and salt
ce:section.ce:para:
Water evaporation systems are usually affected by temperature and cannot be operated effectively at low temperatures. However, the CDs-Wood system exhibited a high-water evaporation rate not only at room temperature but also at low temperatures, as shown in Fig. 5
a, the water evaporation rate of CDs-Wood for pure water was measured to be 1.74 kg m−2 h−1 even at an ambient temperature of only 8 °C. This result indicated that CDs-Wood could be applied in a wider range of temperatures and may can be used effectively in cold winter. The tolerance for low-temperature of the CDs-Wood may be due to the high thermal insulation of the wood, which can effectively reduce the heat loss.

When a common evaporator was applied in seawater, in contrast to pure water, the accumulation of salt on its surface significantly reduced the rate of water evaporation. Some evaporators have demonstrated exceptional salt resistance through their structural design [68,69]. For instance, the Janus hydrogel evaporator, reported by He et al., achieved salt resistance through ion transfer engineering [70]. We conducted experiments to evaluate the salt resistance of CDs-Wood, using both simulated seawater (3.5 % wt NaCl solution) and a saturated sodium chloride solution (26.5 % wt NaCl) at a temperature of 8 °C. As shown in Fig. 5 b, the rate of water evaporation remained consistent (1.74 kg m−2 h−1) among pure water, simulated seawater, and saturated saltwater conditions. The performance of the evaporator was also assessed with real seawater from the Yellow Sea (Qingdao, China), as shown in Fig. 5 c. After continuous tests in the real seawater for 24 h, the evaporation rate maintained stable, and no salt accumulation was observed during the test. This showed the excellent resistance to salt interference exhibited by the CDs-Wood. The stability of the CDs-Wood's evaporation capability in the real seawater was also evaluated by a cycle-testing, the results showed that no significant decrease in the water evaporation rate even after 10 cycles (Fig. 5 d). Biomass-based evaporators may be affected by microbial infections. However, we did not observe mildew growth in the CDs-Wood after floating on pure water or natural seawater for a week ( Fig. S16 ). CDs are commonly acknowledged for their antibacterial property [71], thus we believe that CDs provided an antibacterial effect for the CDs-Wood, thereby the CDs-Wood can remain stable without mildew problems. The remarkable resistance to salt interference in CDs-Wood primarily arises from its anisotropic pore structures and the increased hydrophilicity after hydrothermal treatment (most of the lignin has been converted to hydrophilic carbon dots). When the evaporator was tested in saline water, water was transported to the evaporation interface through these pore structures, and the high concentration of salt at the evaporation interface was transported into the solution due to the concentration gradient. At the same time, the hydrophilicity of CDs-Wood is also conducive to the diffusion of high concentration salt at the evaporation interface and not conducive to the precipitation crystallization of salt. Therefore, the pore structures and hydrophilicity of CDs-Wood effectively prevent salt accumulation at the evaporation interface and resulting in an enhancement of the evaporator's performance.

In summary, we have developed a one-step method that can effectively reduce the latent heat of water evaporation in wood, thereby achieving highly efficient water vapor generation by using renewable and environmentally-friendly wood material. The water evaporation mechanism of CDs-Wood was thoroughly investigated. The polar functional groups on the CDs plays a vital role in reducing hydrogen bonding interactions among water molecules, significantly decreasing the energy required for the transition of water molecules from liquid to gas phase. In addition, owing to the capillary effect by wood, the water present in CDs-Wood can transform into a gaseous state in the form of clusters, thus substantially minimizing the quantity of hydrogen bonds that must be broken during the process of water evaporation. The combined effects of the porous structures of CDs-Wood and the polar functional groups on the surface of CDs thus can reduce the latent heat of water evaporation from 2257.5 to 1003.8 J g−1. The CDs-Wood evaporator exhibits several advantages: firstly, it has high evaporation efficiency, achieving a water evaporation rate of 2.86 kg m−2 h−1 with a solar thermal conversion efficiency of 80 % under 1 sun irradiation and an ambient temperature of 20 °C; secondly, this type of evaporator shows excellent environmental adaptability, maintaining the water evaporation rate of 1.74 kg m−2 h−1 even at a low ambient temperature of 8 °C; additionally, the CDs-Wood has exceptional salt-resistant ability whether applied to seawater or saturated saltwater, and the water evaporation rate remains constant and equivalent to that of pure water. This work provides a novel strategy for using biomass materials to prepare sustainable, efficient, low-cost and easy scale-up solar-driven interface evaporator, which shows the great potential to solve the shortage of freshwater resources.

Materials: Balsa Wood (obtained by longitudinally cutting the wood trunk, purchased from Topsener, China). Lignin, cellulose, xylan and Urea (AR 99 %) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Chemical Co., Ltd.

CDs-Wood was prepared by hydrothermal method. Under continuous stirring, 1 g of urea was completely dissolved in 4 mL of water in a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL), then a piece of wood (1.5 cm × 1.5 cm × 0.5 cm) was added and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the wood was washed with deionized water for three times and air-dried.

To prepare C-CDs, 1 g of urea was first dissolved in 4 mL of water in a Teflon- lined autoclave (25 mL), then 0.1 g of cellulose powder was added and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the liquid was filtered through a 0.22 μm PVDF syringe filter to remove any large particles, then was dialyzed for 48 h (molecular weight cut off = 3.5 kDa), and finally was freeze-dried to obtain C-CDs powder. The L-CDs and H-CDs were also prepared in the same method.

After taking out of the CDs-Wood, the residual suspension in the autoclave from the preparation of CDs-Wood was also filtered through a 0.22 μm PVDF syringe filter to remove any large particles, then was dialyzed for 48 h (molecular weight cut-off = 3.5 kDa), finally, the suspension was freeze-dried to obtain CDs powder.

The morphology of the wood and CDs-Wood were investigated by SEM (SU8010, Hitachi Ltd., Japan), the samples were mounted onto an aluminum stub, coated by a layer of platinum with an Ion Sputter Coater (MC1000, Hitachi Ltd., Japan). The CDs were observed by TEM (HT-7700Exalens, Hitachi Ltd., Japan). To prepare the specimens, the CDs were dispersed in deionized water with a concentration of 1 µg/mL, then 10 µL of the CDs suspension was dropped onto the surface of an ultra-thin carbon coated copper grid and dried completely. The fluorescence spectra of CDs were obtained by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FS5, Edinburgh Ltd., UK). The absorption spectra of CDs and CDs-Wood were obtained by UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (UV-3600 plus, Shimadzu Ltd., Japan). The crystallinity of wood and CDs-Wood were measured by XRD (D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a FTIR spectrometer (VERTEX 70, Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a K-Alpha X-rays (Thermo Fisher Scientific Ltd., USA). The surface temperature of the samples was obtained by an infrared thermal camera (E52, FLIR Ltd., USA). The Raman spectra of water in CDs-wood were recorded on a Raman spectrometer (inVia, Renishaw, UK) with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. The N2 physisorption isotherms at 77 K were operated on Quantachrome Instruments version 3.01. Pore size distribution was calculated by the density functional theory (DFT) model, Barret-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) model or Horvath-Kawazoe (HK) model. Specific surface area (SBET) was calculated by using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) equation. The latent heat of water evaporation analysis was conducted on a Differential Scanning Calorimeter instrument (DSC, 200F3, NETZSCH) with a heating and cooling rate of 5 °C min−1, each sample was placed in analuminum cruciblewitha small holein the lid. We used the mass of water within wood or CDs-Wood for calculating the latent heat of evaporation, and we obtained the mass of water by calculating the mass change of wood or CDs-Wood before and after water absorption. To measure the water transfer capacity from bulk water to CDs-Wood, the dried CDs-Wood was placed on the evaporator setup without any light irradiation, and the mass of the wood was recorded every 10 s by taking it out and weighting it on an electronic balance (BCE224I, Sartorius, Germany). The mass vs. time were plotted, and the slope of this fitted line divided by the contact area will be the water transfer rate. Solar evaporation tests were performed using a solar simulator (CEL-S500, China Education Au-light Ltd., China) with an outputting solar flux at 1 kW m−2 (equivalent to 1 sun irradiation). The light intensity was calibrated before each test by a thermal power sensor (S401C, Thorlabs Ltd., USA) attached to a handheld digital power meter (PM100, Thorlabs Ltd., USA). To measure the water evaporation rate, the mass changes were automatically recorded by an electronic balance (BCE224I, Sartorius, Germany).
